title: Lyrids
visible_in: Visible worldwide; best from the Northern Hemisphere where the radiant climbs higher before dawn.
moonlight_note: Moon ~27% — generally good dark-sky conditions after midnight.
description: A classic spring shower with medium-fast meteors and occasional bright fireballs. Best after midnight as the radiant rises higher. No equipment needed—use a dark site and give your eyes 20–30 minutes to adapt.

title: Eta Aquariids
visible_in: Best from the Southern Hemisphere and tropics (radiant higher before dawn). Northern Hemisphere sees lower rates.
moonlight_note: Moon ~84% — bright moonlight will wash out many fainter meteors.
description: Very fast meteors from Halley’s Comet debris, often with persistent trains. Best in the hours before dawn. Choose the darkest sky you can—this year moonlight will reduce the visible count.

title: Southern Delta Aquariids
visible_in: Best from the Southern Hemisphere and tropics; radiant stays low from higher northern latitudes.
moonlight_note: Moon ~98% — near full Moon, strongly reduced visibility.
description: A steady summer shower that can reward patient watching over multiple nights. Best after midnight toward dawn. This year’s near-full Moon will hide many fainter meteors—dark skies still help.

title: Alpha Capricornids
visible_in: Visible from both hemispheres; best where the radiant gets higher late at night (often good from the tropics).
moonlight_note: Moon ~98% — near full Moon, only brighter meteors/fireballs likely to stand out.
description: Not a high-rate shower, but famous for occasional bright fireballs. Best late night to pre-dawn. With a bright Moon this year, treat it as a ‘fireball watch’ rather than a count-heavy shower.

title: Perseids
visible_in: Best from the Northern Hemisphere (radiant high). Still visible elsewhere, but typically fewer meteors.
moonlight_note: Moon ~0% — near new Moon, ideal dark skies.
description: The famous summer shower with many bright, fast meteors and occasional fireballs. Best after midnight to dawn when the radiant is higher. This is a standout year thanks to moonless skies—go somewhere dark.

title: Draconids
visible_in: Best from the Northern Hemisphere; often an evening shower (a rare exception).
moonlight_note: Moon conditions vary by exact hour; best later if the Moon sets early for your location.
description: An evening-timed shower that can be quiet most years but occasionally surprises with outbursts. Watch soon after dusk when the radiant is up. A dark sky is key, and expectations should be modest unless an outburst is forecast.

title: Orionids
visible_in: Visible worldwide; best after midnight when Orion climbs higher (especially good from mid-latitudes).
moonlight_note: Moon ~80% — bright moonlight reduces the visible rate; darkest window is near dawn if the Moon is low/setting.
description: Fast meteors from Halley’s Comet debris, sometimes with bright fireballs. Best after midnight toward dawn. Moonlight is strong this year, so focus on the darkest hours and get away from light pollution.

title: Southern Taurids
visible_in: Visible from both hemispheres; tends to reward patient watching with slow, bright meteors and occasional fireballs.
moonlight_note: Moon ~18% — generally favorable conditions for fireball spotting.
description: Low overall rates, but famous for slow, bright meteors and occasional fireballs. Best late night. Treat it as ‘fireball season’ rather than a high-count shower—dark skies improve the odds.

title: Northern Taurids
visible_in: Visible widely; radiant tends to be higher for Northern Hemisphere observers, but fireballs can appear anywhere in the sky.
moonlight_note: Moon ~7% — very dark conditions.
description: Another low-rate Taurid branch known for slow meteors and occasional bright fireballs. Best late night. With dark skies this year, it’s a strong ‘fireball watch’ window if clouds cooperate.

title: Leonids
visible_in: Visible worldwide; often best in the pre-dawn hours when the radiant is higher.
moonlight_note: Moon around mid-phase (~45%) — fainter meteors reduced, but brighter ones still visible from dark sites.
description: A famous shower historically linked to meteor storms, but most years are modest. Best before dawn in dark skies. Expect fewer meteors than Perseids/Geminids, but occasional bright streaks are possible.

title: Geminids
visible_in: Visible worldwide and often productive even before midnight—one of the best showers for casual viewers.
moonlight_note: Moon ~21% — generally good conditions, especially after it sets (location-dependent).
description: Often the strongest reliable annual shower with many bright meteors. Unlike many showers, it can be great from late evening onward. Wrap up warm, pick a dark site, and watch a wide area of sky.

title: Ursids
visible_in: Best from the Northern Hemisphere (radiant far north; often circumpolar).
moonlight_note: Moon ~94% — bright moonlight will hide many faint Ursids.
description: A modest late-December shower with occasional surprises. Best before dawn and favored by northern observers. With strong moonlight this year, expect only brighter meteors unless skies are very dark.

title: Quadrantids
visible_in: Strongly Northern Hemisphere favored (radiant far north). Best in the hours before dawn.
moonlight_note: Moon ~13% — good dark skies for the short peak.
description: A potentially very strong shower with a narrow peak that can be easy to miss if you don’t watch at the right time. Best before dawn under dark skies. Dress warm and watch a wide field of view.

title: Lyrids
visible_in: Visible worldwide; typically better for Northern Hemisphere observers before dawn.
moonlight_note: Near full Moon period in many 2027 calendars — expect reduced counts unless the Moon is low/setting.
description: A reliable spring shower with occasional bright meteors. Best after midnight toward dawn. If moonlight is strong, focus on the darkest part of the night and get away from light pollution.

title: Eta Aquariids
visible_in: Best from the Southern Hemisphere and tropics before dawn; northern observers see fewer meteors.
moonlight_note: Near new Moon in many 2027 forecasts — very favorable dark skies.
description: Fast meteors from Halley’s Comet debris, often with long trains. Best in the pre-dawn hours when the radiant is higher. Dark skies + clear weather make a big difference.

title: Southern Delta Aquariids
visible_in: Best from the Southern Hemisphere / tropics. Northern mid-latitudes see fewer because the radiant stays lower.
moonlight_note: Near new Moon in many 2027 forecasts — excellent dark skies.
description: A steady mid-summer shower best after midnight to dawn. It doesn’t always have a sharp single-hour peak, so watching multiple nights can pay off. Dark skies and clear weather matter most.

title: Perseids
visible_in: Best from the Northern Hemisphere (radiant high). Still visible elsewhere at lower rates.
moonlight_note: Near full Moon period in many 2027 forecasts — bright moonlight reduces visible meteors.
description: A major summer shower with many bright, fast meteors. Best after midnight to dawn. If the Moon is bright, focus on the darkest hours and get away from city lights—expect fewer faint meteors.

title: Draconids
visible_in: Best from the Northern Hemisphere; often best soon after dusk (evening shower).
moonlight_note: Moon near first-quarter period in many 2027 outlooks; better later if the Moon sets for your location.
description: An evening shower that’s usually modest, but sometimes produces surprise outbursts. Watch after dusk with a wide view of the sky. Keep expectations realistic unless an outburst is predicted.

title: Orionids
visible_in: Visible worldwide; best after midnight toward dawn as Orion climbs higher.
moonlight_note: Last-quarter style conditions in many 2027 outlooks — typically less interference than full Moon.
description: Fast meteors from Halley’s Comet debris, sometimes with bright fireballs. Best after midnight. A dark site boosts your meteor count far more than any equipment—wide sky coverage wins.

title: Southern Taurids
visible_in: Visible widely; low-rate but known for slow, bright meteors and occasional fireballs.
moonlight_note: Near full Moon period in many 2027 outlooks — bright sky reduces visibility.
description: Not about big numbers—this is ‘fireball season.’ Watch late night with a wide view of the sky. With strong moonlight expected in many locations, focus on brighter meteors and darker observing hours.

title: Northern Taurids
visible_in: Visible widely; radiant often higher for Northern Hemisphere mid-latitudes.
moonlight_note: Near full Moon period in many 2027 outlooks — fewer faint meteors visible.
description: Low-rate, slow meteors with occasional bright events. Best late night. If the Moon is bright, focus on fireballs and the darkest hours available—dark sites still help a lot.

title: Leonids
visible_in: Visible worldwide; usually best before dawn when the radiant is higher.
moonlight_note: Moon often around last-quarter style conditions in many 2027 outlooks; interference depends on when the Moon rises/sets locally.
description: A historic shower famous for rare storm years, but typically modest today. Best before dawn under dark skies. Even a handful of bright Leonids can be a great session if the sky is clear.

title: Geminids
visible_in: Visible worldwide; often excellent from late evening onward (not just pre-dawn).
moonlight_note: Moon ~2 days past full at peak — strong interference all night.
description: Usually the strongest annual shower, but this year moonlight will overpower many faint meteors. Still worth watching from a dark site—focus on brighter Geminids and longer sessions late evening through dawn.

title: Ursids
visible_in: Best from the Northern Hemisphere (radiant far north; often circumpolar).
moonlight_note: Near new Moon in many 2027 outlooks — dark skies help this modest shower.
description: A modest but enjoyable late-December shower, best before dawn and favored by northern observers. Dark skies can make a big difference for a lower-rate shower. Great as a ‘holiday skywatch’ session if weather cooperates.
